perturbation.original_text: "Trade Secrets" means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically or in writing, to the extent that the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret and the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, the public.
perturbation.changed_text: "Trade Secrets" means information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically or in writing, regardless of whether the owner has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret and regardless of whether the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, the public.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirements that the owner take 'reasonable measures to keep such information secret' and that the information derive 'independent economic value' the definition of 'trade secrets' is broadened in a way that conflicts with standard legal definitions.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text removes two key requirements for information to be considered a trade secret under the UTSA: reasonable measures to maintain secrecy and independent economic value. This contradiction could render purported 'trade secrets' unprotected under the law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirements for 'reasonable measures to keep such information secret' and 'independent economic value' for something to be considered a trade secret. The Perturbation Explanation states that this broadened definition conflicts with standard legal definitions. The Law Explanation confirms that this contradicts the UTSA, potentially rendering purported 'trade secrets' unprotected. While the provided law snippets are unhelpful, the removal of these elements directly contradicts the standard definition of trade secrets, creating a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: "Intellectual Property Rights" or "IPR" means any and all intellectual property rights existing anywhere in the world associated with any and all (a) Patents, (b) Trademarks, (c) Copyrights, (d) Domain Names, (e) Software, (f) Trade Secrets and other confidential information, (g) all tangible embodiments of the foregoing in whatever form or medium and (h) any other legal protections and rights related to any of the foregoing. Intellectual Property Rights specifically excludes contractual rights (including license grants from third parties).
perturbation.changed_text: "Intellectual Property Rights" or "IPR" means any and all intellectual property rights existing anywhere in the world associated with any and all (a) Patents, (b) Trademarks, (c) Copyrights, (d) Domain Names, (e) Software, (f) Trade Secrets and other confidential information, (g) all tangible embodiments of the foregoing in whatever form or medium and (h) any other legal protections and rights related to any of the foregoing. Intellectual Property Rights specifically includes contractual rights (including license grants from third parties).
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the scope of Intellectual Property Rights to *include* contractual rights, when the original version specifically excludes it. This change directly impacts the interpretation and enforcement of the entire contract, as it changes the kinds of rights that fall under the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Varies based on specific jurisdiction, but generally contract law principles related to intellectual property.
perturbation.law_explanation: This contradicts the established legal principle that ownership of intellectual property rights (e.g., copyright) is distinct from contractual licenses to use those rights. Section 201(d) outlines how ownership can be transferred, which is separate from granting a license. This change creates ambiguity by conflating ownership with mere contractual usage rights, potentially leading to disputes over control and enforcement of IP.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Initial Ownership.— Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire.— In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright. (c) Contributions to Collective Works.— Copyright in each separate contribution to a collective work is distinct from copyright in the collective work as a whole, and vests initially in the author of the contribution. In the absence of an express transfer of the copyright or of any rights under it, the owner of copyright in the collective work is presumed to have acquired only the privilege of reproducing and distributing the contribution as part of that particular collective work, any revision of that collective work, and any later collective work in the same series. (d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession. (2) Any of the exclusive rights comprised in a copyright, including any subdivision of any of the rights specified by section 106, may be transferred as provided by clause (1) and owned separately. The owner of any particular exclusive right is entitled, to the extent of that right, to all of the protection and remedies accorded to the copyright owner by this title. perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original definition of "Intellectual Property Rights" by including contractual rights. The law snippets, specifically 17 U.S. Code § 201, outline the ownership of copyright and how it can be transferred, which is distinct from granting a license. By including contractual rights within the definition of IPR, the changed text blurs the lines between ownership and contractual usage, potentially leading to disputes over control and enforcement of intellectual property. The inclusion of contractual rights where they were explicitly excluded creates a misaligned terminology legal contradiction, as the contract deviates from legally recognized definitions regarding the ownership and transfer of copyright.

perturbation.original_text: "Software" means any and all (a) computer programs and applications, including any and all software implementations of algorithms, models and methodologies, whether in source code, object code, human readable form or other form, (b) databases and compilations, including any and all data and collections of data, whether machine readable or otherwise, (c) descriptions, flow charts and other work product used to design, plan, organize and develop any of the foregoing, screens, user interfaces, report formats, firmware, development tools, templates, menus, buttons and icons, (d) all documentation including user manuals and other training documentation related to any of the foregoing and (e) all tangible embodiments of the foregoing in whatever form or medium now known or yet to be created, including all disks, diskettes and tapes.
perturbation.changed_text: "Software" means any and all (a) computer programs and applications, including any and all software implementations of algorithms, models and methodologies, whether in source code, object code, human readable form or other form, (b) databases and compilations, including any and all data and collections of data, whether machine readable or otherwise, (c) descriptions, flow charts and other work product used to design, plan, organize and develop any of the foregoing, screens, user interfaces, report formats, firmware, development tools, templates, menus, buttons and icons, (d) all documentation including user manuals and other training documentation related to any of the foregoing and (e) all tangible embodiments of the foregoing in whatever form or medium now known or yet to be created, including all disks, diskettes and tapes. Software excludes operating systems.
perturbation.explanation: The modification excludes 'operating systems' from the definition of software. Copyright law protects operating systems as software. By excluding them from the contract's definition, the agreement potentially fails to address rights and obligations related to operating systems used by both parties.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Copyright Act defines computer programs (which includes operating systems) as a set of statements or instructions to be used directly or indirectly in a computer to bring about a certain result. By excluding operating systems from the contract's definition of 'software', the agreement creates ambiguity and could potentially lead to disputes regarding copyright protection, licensing, and usage rights related to operating systems.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 U.S. Code § 101 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 101 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next Except as otherwise provided in this title, as used in this title, the following terms and their variant forms mean the following: An “ anonymous work ” is a work on the copies or phonorecords of which no natural person is identified as author. An “ architectural work ” is the design of a building as embodied in any tangible medium of expression, including a building, architectural plans, or drawings. The work includes the overall form as well as the arrangement and composition of spaces and elements in the design, but does not include individual standard features. “ Audiovisual works ” are works that consist of a series of related images which are intrinsically intended to be shown by the use of machines, or devices such as projectors, viewers, or electronic equipment, together with accompanying sounds, if any, regardless of the nature of the material objects, such as films or tapes, in which the works are embodied. The “ Berne Convention ” is the Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works, signed at Berne, Switzerland, on September 9, 1886 , and all acts, protocols, and revisions thereto. The “ best edition ” of a work is the edition, published in the United States at any time before the date of deposit, that the Library of Congress determines to be most suitable for its purposes. A person’s “ children ” are that person’s immediate offspring, whether legitimate or not, and any children legally adopted by that person. A “ collective work ” is a work, such as a periodical issue, anthology, or encyclopedia, in which a number of contributions, constituting separate and independent works in themselves, are assembled into a collective whole. perturbation.justification: The modified text excludes 'operating systems' from the definition of 'Software'. The Law Explanation states that the Copyright Act defines computer programs (including operating systems) as sets of statements or instructions used in computers. By excluding operating systems, the contract creates ambiguity regarding copyright, licensing, and usage rights, which contradicts the legally recognized definition of software and copyright protection as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The modification aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.
